title.title: Critical Thinking
title.subtitle: When to use a smart contract — and when not to. Adoption considerations for serious business deployment.

decisionFlow.heading: Should you use a smart contract?
decisionFlow.subtitle: Answer 8 questions about your use case. Get a verdict — and the reasoning behind it.

questions.parties: Multiple distrusting parties need shared data?
questions.auto: Does automation create significant value (cost, speed, fraud)?
questions.inter: Are intermediary costs high or disintermediation desirable?
questions.immut: Is immutability a feature, not a bug?
questions.trans: Is transparency important to all stakeholders?
questions.priv: Is privacy paramount (GDPR, HIPAA, sensitive data)?
questions.reg: Is your regulatory environment hostile or unclear?
questions.flex: Do you need flexibility to change rules frequently?

sc.title: Smart Contract
sc.desc: Your use case fits the smart-contract pattern. Multi-party, automation-heavy, immutability-friendly. Proceed — but audit obsessively.

hybrid.title: Hybrid Approach
hybrid.desc: Pure on-chain is risky here. Use a hybrid: on-chain hashes + off-chain encrypted data, or permissioned chain with off-chain governance overrides.

db.title: Traditional DB
db.desc: A traditional database (with audit logs and proper access control) will be faster, cheaper, and more flexible. Skip blockchain — you don't need it.

decisionIntro.heading: The Most Important Question
decisionIntro.subtitle: Before writing a single line of Solidity, answer this honestly.
decisionIntro.card2: Not every blockchain needs smart contracts.
decisionIntro.footnote: Build a decision framework. Develop critical thinking. Next: the canonical decision tree — then our interactive version.

decisionChart.heading: The Decision Tree
decisionChart.subtitle: Walk down the questions. Each branch eliminates a category — only one path lands on a smart contract.
decisionChart.imgAlt: Decision tree starting at 'Do you need a shared database?'. NO → Traditional Database. YES → 'Do multiple parties need write access?' NO → Traditional Database. YES → 'Do the parties fully trust each other?' YES → Traditional Database with Access Controls. NO → 'Is there a trusted third party everyone accepts?' YES → Third-Party Managed Database. NO → 'Do you need complex business logic?' NO → Simple Blockchain (Bitcoin-like). YES → Smart Contract Platform.

steps.q: A trusted third party everyone accepts?

decisionChart.pointBody: most paths end at a database. Smart contracts are the answer only when every other option falls short.

teamFramework.title: Run your problem through the framework
teamFramework.subtitle: Take the problem statement you brought from Day 2 and answer the 8 questions on the previous slide — together, as a team. Treat this as a real go/no-go gate for your project.

method.a: Open the interactive decision flow on the previous slide. Answer each question
Disagree as a team if you must — note the disagreements as open questions to research.
Record your verdict: ✅ Smart Contract · ⚠️ Hybrid · ❌ Database.

discuss.b: : figure out what part is on-chain vs off-chain. Most real-world deployments live here.
: that's a perfectly valid project too. Your deliverable becomes
discuss.em: "we investigated X and concluded blockchain isn't the right tool because Y, Z"
discuss.c: — backed by the analysis. The rubric rewards critical thinking, not blockchain enthusiasm.

capture.a: One specific risk the framework surfaced (privacy concern? flexibility need? single trusted party lurking?).

footnote.a: if your team can't convince a sceptical reviewer in two sentences
footnote.b: blockchain is the right tool, the verdict probably isn't ✅ — and that's fine. A clear-eyed "no, here's why" earns the same Problem-Solution-fit points as a strong "yes". The Three Lessons coming up (Estonia / The DAO / ASX) make all three outcomes concrete.

decisionYes.heading: When Smart Contracts Make Sense ✅
decisionYes.subtitle: If most of these are true, blockchain is worth the engineering cost.

items.title: Multiple parties with trust issues
items.desc: No single entity is trusted by all. A shared, neutral source of truth pays for itself.

items.title: Automation creates significant value
items.desc: Manual reconciliation, escrow, or settlement is the bottleneck — not just a nuisance.

items.title: Transparency matters to stakeholders
items.desc: Customers, regulators, or investors need to verify behavior themselves — not take your word for it.

items.title: Intermediary costs are high
items.desc: Brokers, lawyers, escrow agents, clearinghouses — disintermediation produces real savings.

items.title: Disintermediation is desirable
items.desc: Removing a gatekeeper improves the product (e.g. global DEXs vs centralized brokers).

items.title: Immutability is a feature
items.desc: Audit trails, ownership records, votes — you WANT them to be unchangeable forever.

items.title: Asset ownership needs to be portable
items.desc: Users move between platforms, jurisdictions, or wallets. Their assets shouldn't be locked in.

decisionNo.heading: When to Rethink Smart Contracts ❌
decisionNo.subtitle: Any of these is a red flag. Multiple = a database is almost certainly the better answer.

items.title: Single party can be trusted
items.desc: You ARE the trusted intermediary — like a stock exchange clearing trades. A database is simpler.

items.title: Centralized is cheaper and faster
items.desc: For internal workflows, a Postgres database with proper audit logs beats a blockchain on every metric.

items.title: Flexibility to change rules is critical
items.desc: If business logic evolves weekly, immutability becomes a liability rather than an asset.

items.title: Off-chain data drives most logic
items.desc: If 80% of your inputs come from oracles or APIs, the trust assumption sits there — not on-chain.

items.title: Privacy is paramount
items.desc: Public chains expose everything. GDPR, HIPAA, trade secrets — these conflict directly with public ledgers.

items.title: Regulatory environment is hostile
items.desc: If your jurisdiction prohibits or heavily restricts on-chain operations, fighting that is a strategic mistake.

items.title: Users aren't tech-savvy enough
items.desc: Wallet UX, gas fees, seed phrases — these still cripple adoption for mainstream consumers.

items.title: No one will pay for development
items.desc: Smart contract dev + audit costs are 5–10× a typical web app. If there's no clear ROI, don't start.

casesIntro.heading: Three Lessons from the Field
casesIntro.subtitle: The decision framework on the previous slides is abstract. These three real projects make it concrete — one per outcome.

items.name: Estonia · e-Residency
items.desc: Government as the trust anchor + blockchain for record integrity. The use case fit perfectly: many users, need for tamper-proof history, hybrid of institutional trust and mathematical guarantees.
items.lesson: When the framework says yes AND the organization is ready, blockchain delivers durable value at national scale.

items.name: The DAO
items.desc: A legitimate DAO use case (decentralised VC) but the smart contract had a known reentrancy bug. Once exploited, immutability meant nothing could pause it. Ethereum forked to recover funds.
items.lesson: "Code is law" cuts both ways. Without audits, kill-switches, or upgrade paths, immutability becomes liability — not feature.

items.name: ASX · CHESS
items.desc: Securities clearing is run by a single trusted intermediary (ASX itself). Blockchain solves multi-party trust gaps — but ASX was the trusted party. A database would have been faster, cheaper, simpler.
items.lesson: Asking "who do we not trust?" before "how do we use blockchain?" prevents $255M mistakes. Hype is not a justification.

casesIntro.patternBody: the three cases differ on two axes — was this the right tool for the problem, and was the organisation ready to wield it. Both must be true for success.

caseSuccess.kicker: Lesson 01 · ✅ Success — right tool, right organisation
caseSuccess.heading: Estonia · e-Residency
caseSuccess.subtitle: Blockchain-backed digital identity since 2014. The framework says yes — and the organisation was ready to deliver.
background: Built on KSI blockchain (Guardtime) for record integrity
National ID infrastructure → digital signatures with legal weight
X-Road system connects state databases for citizens and residents
useCases: Sign documents legally — any contract, anywhere
Establish an EU company without setting foot in Estonia
Healthcare records integrity assured via on-chain anchors
caseSuccess.whyBody: government as the trust anchor + blockchain for record integrity. Hybrid of institutional trust and mathematical guarantees.

caseScFail.imgAlt: The DAO timeline: idea → launch → hack → fork
caseScFail.kicker: Lesson 02 · ⚠️ Smart-contract failure — right tool, wrong contract design
caseScFail.heading: The DAO · Decentralized Autonomous Organization
caseScFail.subtitle: $150M raised, $60M drained. The use case fit blockchain. The contract didn't.
vision: $150M raised via crowdfunding — largest crowdfund of its time
Governed entirely by smart contract — no executives, no board
Token holders vote on investment proposals
Promised: democratized VC, global, transparent collaboration
hack: Reentrancy vulnerability in the withdraw function
Contract was immutable — couldn't be paused or patched
"Child DAO" delay gave the community 27 days to react
response: Ethereum community executed a hard fork to reverse the attack
Chain split into Ethereum (ETH, post-fork) and Ethereum Classic (ETC, original)
"Code is law" — rejected when stakes were existential
Governance proved messier than the contract assumed
caseScFail.lessonsBody: immutability is a double-edged sword. Audit obsessively. Have a kill-switch, multisig, or upgrade pattern for safety-critical contracts.

caseOrgFail.imgAlt: ASIC vs ASX with a gavel
caseOrgFail.kicker: Lesson 03 · ❌ Organisational failure — wrong tool entirely
caseOrgFail.heading: ASX · CHESS Replacement Project
caseOrgFail.subtitle: $255M+ AUD spent, six years of work — abandoned in 2022. The framework would have flagged this on day one.
background: ASX (Australian Securities Exchange) launched the project in 2017
Goal: replace 25-year-old CHESS clearing system with blockchain
happened: Cancelled in November 2022 after 6 years of work
System failed to meet performance and functional requirements
Fell behind faster, cheaper traditional database alternatives
Major reputational hit to ASX and the entire enterprise blockchain narrative
why: Complexity dramatically underestimated — production-grade DLT is hard
A traditional database would have been faster and cheaper
Stakeholder misalignment between ASX, brokers, and tech vendor
"Blockchain for blockchain's sake" — the underlying need didn't actually require DLT
caseOrgFail.lessonBody: just because you CAN use blockchain doesn't mean you SHOULD. The discipline of asking "is a database better?" matters more than the technology hype.

daoQuiz.question: The DAO hack of 2016 drained $60M via a reentrancy bug. The Ethereum community executed a hard fork to reverse the attack — splitting the chain into ETH and ETC. What is the most important LESSON for smart contract designers?
options: "Code is law" is a flawed principle — when stakes are high enough, social consensus overrides immutability.
Reentrancy attacks are impossible to prevent — every contract is fundamentally vulnerable.
The Ethereum Foundation should be allowed to reverse all major exploits to protect users.
Decentralized organizations are technically impossible and should not be attempted.
daoQuiz.explanation: The DAO hack exposed a tension that still defines blockchain governance: pure immutability vs human values. The Ethereum community chose to fork because letting an attacker keep $60M was unacceptable — but doing so undermined the 'code is law' ideal that blockchain claims. The lesson for builders: 1) audit obsessively before deploying, especially safety-critical contracts; 2) consider upgrade patterns or kill-switches for value-holding contracts; 3) understand that 'immutability' is a feature with limits — extreme failures will be socially renegotiated. Reentrancy is preventable (Checks-Effects-Interactions, ReentrancyGuard); the DAO bug was a known anti-pattern, just deployed at scale.

asxQuiz.question: The Australian Securities Exchange (ASX) spent $255M+ AUD over 6 years trying to replace its CHESS clearing system with blockchain — and cancelled the project in 2022. What is the most likely root cause of this failure?
options: Blockchain technology is fundamentally incapable of handling securities clearing.
The chosen vendor (Digital Asset Holdings) was technically incompetent.
The use case did not actually require blockchain — a traditional database would have been simpler, faster, and cheaper.
Australian regulators forced the project to be abandoned for political reasons.
asxQuiz.explanation: The ASX CHESS replacement is the canonical case of 'blockchain for blockchain\'s sake.' CHESS is operated by a single trusted entity (ASX) with a small known set of participants (brokers). Blockchain shines when you have multiple distrusting parties needing a single source of truth without a trusted intermediary — but ASX IS the trusted intermediary, by mandate. A modern database with proper audit logs would have delivered better performance, faster development, and lower cost. The lesson: before choosing blockchain, ask 'who do we not trust?' If the answer is 'no one — we are the trusted party,' a database is almost always better. Hype is not a justification.

adoptionIntro.heading: Key Considerations for Business Adoption
adoptionIntro.subtitle: Introducing smart contracts isn't just a technology project — it's an organizational change.

pillars.label: Strategic Alignment
pillars.desc: Use case clarity, success metrics, exec buy-in, staff training

pillars.label: Technical Infrastructure
pillars.desc: Scalable platform, legacy integration, IT readiness

pillars.label: Risk Management
pillars.desc: Audits, incident response, contract immutability planning

pillars.label: Financial Planning
pillars.desc: Upfront costs, ongoing gas, ROI timeline

adoptionIntro.footnote: Smart Contracts have huge market potential. But they are not plug-and-play — businesses need strategic purpose, infrastructure, skills, compliance, risk controls, and resources. The next 5 chapters cover each pillar.

adoptionStrategy.heading: Strategic Alignment
adoptionStrategy.subtitle: Adopting smart contracts must serve a clear business purpose and align with company strategy.

items.title: Clarify the use case
items.desc: Pinpoint where a smart contract solves a real pain point — automating a manual workflow, removing a costly intermediary, or building shared truth across distrusting parties. Align this with the company's strategic priorities — not the other way around.

items.title: Define success metrics
items.desc: Set specific, measurable targets: cost reduction (e.g. 30% in dispute resolution), speed improvement (e.g. days → minutes), error rate reduction. Forms the basis of a business case for stakeholders.

items.title: Secure executive buy-in
items.desc: Demonstrate how smart contracts support the broader business vision — disintermediation, transparency, automation. Without C-suite support, projects stall when the first technical hurdle hits.

items.title: Train staff
items.desc: Educate employees about blockchain basics and the specifics of the chosen platform/tooling. Provide hands-on training for those directly using or supporting smart contracts. This is non-trivial — budget for it.

adoptionTech.heading: Technical Infrastructure
adoptionTech.subtitle: Implementing smart contracts demands a solid technical foundation — platform, integration, and IT capacity.

items.title: Scalable platform
items.desc: Choose a chain that meets your throughput, latency, and finality needs (Section 6 covers this in depth). EVM compatibility = vast tooling ecosystem.

items.title: Legacy integration
items.desc: Smart contracts must talk to ERPs, CRMs, and SaaS systems. Plan middleware (The Graph, Moralis), event listeners, and data sync from day one.

items.title: IT capacity & ops
items.desc: You need on-call engineers who understand blockchain incident response. Hiring or training them takes months — start before you deploy.

items.title: Identity & access
items.desc: On-chain != anonymous in business contexts. Plan KYC, role-based access, and key management (HSMs, multisigs) ahead of any real deployment.

items.title: Data architecture
items.desc: Decide on-chain vs off-chain split early. Sensitive data NEVER lives on a public chain — use hashes + IPFS + encryption.

items.title: Monitoring & analytics
items.desc: Block explorers, indexers, and dashboards (Dune, The Graph) — these are the prod tools. Set them up like you would any SRE stack.

adoptionLegal.heading: Legal & Regulatory
adoptionLegal.subtitle: Smart contracts blend code and law — raising challenges around enforceability, jurisdiction, and compliance.
adoptionLegal.engageLabel: Things to engage legal counsel on early
engage: Is this contract a "contract" under your jurisdiction's law?
Does the token issued constitute a security (Howey test in US, MiCA in EU)?
How do you handle disputes? Is there a "kill switch" or arbitration mechanism?
What jurisdictions can users come from? KYC/AML implications?
Data privacy: does the chain store anything that triggers GDPR?
Tax treatment: where does revenue accrue? Where do users owe taxes?
adoptionLegal.crossBody: With global transactions, what's valid in one country may be illegal in another. A token sale legal in Singapore may attract SEC enforcement in the US. A contract enforceable in the EU may be unenforceable in countries with no DLT framework.
crossPoints: Geo-fence cautiously — sanctions compliance is non-negotiable
EU MiCA (2024) sets a global precedent — expect emulation
"Code is law" was rejected by courts in The DAO — plan for human override paths
adoptionLegal.practicalBody: early legal review is cheaper than late legal fixes. Engage counsel before you write the first line of contract code.

adoptionRisk.heading: Risk Management
adoptionRisk.subtitle: Once deployed, smart contracts are typically immutable and execute automatically. There is little room for error.

items.title: Pre-deployment audits
items.desc: At least one — ideally two independent — security audits before mainnet. Budget $20k–$100k+ depending on contract complexity.

items.title: Formal verification
items.desc: For high-value contracts, formal verification (Certora, Halmos) proves properties mathematically — beyond what audits catch.

items.title: Bug bounty program
items.desc: Immunefi and similar programs offer ongoing crowd-sourced security review. Treat bounties as a cost of doing business.

items.title: Upgrade pattern (carefully)
items.desc: Proxy contracts allow logic upgrades — but introduce admin keys. Use only when necessary; protect with timelocks and multisig.

items.title: Incident response plan
items.desc: When (not if) something goes wrong, who pauses the contract, contacts users, communicates with media, and coordinates with exchanges?

items.title: Monitor in production
items.desc: Set up real-time alerts for suspicious flows, unusual gas patterns, or governance attacks. Forta, OpenZeppelin Defender automate this.

adoptionFinance.heading: Financial / Resource Planning
adoptionFinance.subtitle: Smart contract adoption demands solid financial planning. Long-term automation savings can be significant — but upfront costs are real.

upfront.label: Infrastructure setup
upfront.desc: Nodes, monitoring, deployment pipeline, dev environments

upfront.label: Smart contract development
upfront.desc: Architecture, Solidity dev, testing — depends heavily on complexity

upfront.label: Security audits
upfront.desc: Independent firms (Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin, ConsenSys Diligence)

upfront.label: Legal & regulatory consultation
upfront.desc: Especially for regulated industries — financial, healthcare, government

ongoing.label: Gas fees
ongoing.desc: Pay-per-transaction; can spike 10–100× during congestion. L2s help significantly.

ongoing.label: Node infrastructure
ongoing.desc: Self-hosted full nodes, or managed (Infura, Alchemy, QuickNode)

ongoing.label: Maintenance & monitoring
ongoing.desc: On-call engineers, alerts, incident response retainer

ongoing.label: Bug bounty program
ongoing.desc: Funded reserve to pay out responsibly disclosed vulnerabilities

ongoing.label: Compliance & reporting
ongoing.desc: KYC providers, AML monitoring, tax reporting tools

adoptionFinance.footnote: Ranges are illustrative and vary widely by region, complexity, and specific requirements. Use them as a starting point for conversations with vendors and finance.

opportunities.heading: Opportunities
opportunities.subtitle: Where smart contracts demonstrably outperform legacy approaches — backed by academic research.

operational.label: Cost reduction
operational.desc: Eliminate intermediaries, automate manual reconciliation, reduce escrow and dispute costs

operational.label: Speed of execution
operational.desc: Settle in seconds rather than days. Cross-border value moves at internet speed.

operational.label: Transparency & auditability
operational.desc: Every state change is recorded permanently — regulatory examinations become simpler.

operational.label: Programmable cash flows
operational.desc: Encode business rules directly into payments — escrow, royalties, and milestones automate themselves.

strategic.label: New business models
strategic.desc: DAOs, tokenized assets, fractional ownership, and machine-to-machine payments — none possible without smart contracts.

strategic.label: Network effects
strategic.desc: Composability: each new contract amplifies the value of every other contract on the same chain.

strategic.label: Global reach
strategic.desc: Permissionless markets reach users in any jurisdiction with internet — no banking partnerships required.

strategic.label: Trust without intermediaries
strategic.desc: Multi-party agreements between strangers — fundamentally new economic primitive.

opportunities.sources: Sources: Nzuva (2019); Perlman (2019); Dal Mas, Dicuonzo, Massaro & Dell'Atti (2019).

challenges.heading: Challenges
challenges.subtitle: The systemic obstacles to mainstream smart contract adoption — as documented in research.

items.title: Technical complexity
items.desc: Solidity, EVM gas mechanics, and asynchronous execution flatten the developer pool. Few engineers know this stack deeply.

items.title: Regulatory uncertainty
items.desc: Securities, tax, KYC, and consumer-protection laws are still evolving. Compliance is a moving target across jurisdictions.

items.title: Security vulnerabilities
items.desc: Reentrancy, oracle manipulation, and access-control bugs have led to billions in losses. Audits are mandatory, not optional.

items.title: Scalability limits
items.desc: Public L1s cap throughput. L2s help but introduce their own complexity (sequencers, fraud proofs, withdrawal delays).

items.title: Interoperability
items.desc: Cross-chain bridges remain fragile and have been a major source of exploits. Single-chain ecosystems are siloed.

items.title: User experience
items.desc: Wallet setup, key management, gas fees, and confirmation times all create friction that mainstream users won't tolerate.

challenges.sources: Sources: Khan et al. (2021); Virani (2024); Perlman (2019).

strategies.heading: Strategies for Competitive Advantage
strategies.subtitle: How real companies turn smart contracts into a durable strategic moat — applied examples.

items.name: Compound
items.desc: Eliminates intermediaries; on-chain interest rates set algorithmically. Capital efficiency that legacy banks cannot match.

items.name: We.trade
items.desc: Bank consortium platform built on Hyperledger Fabric for SME cross-border trade. Eventually shut down — illustrating that "consortium" governance is hard.

items.name: Uniswap
items.desc: Anyone can list any token instantly. AMM model removes order-book complexity. Deepest moat: composability with the rest of DeFi.

items.name: Propy
items.desc: NFT deeds for legitimate property sales. Compresses 30-day closing into hours where law allows.

items.name: AXA Fizzy
items.desc: Flight delay insurance with automatic payouts via Ethereum + flight oracle. Eventually wound down — but proved the parametric pattern.

items.name: Home Depot
items.desc: Hyperledger Fabric for supplier disputes. Reduced resolution time from weeks to hours — competitive advantage in operating margin.

quiz.question: A bank wants to issue digital bonds. Their internal audit team holds the master record. They have 5 trusted institutional buyers, all KYC'd. Settlement is currently T+2. Which is the strongest reason to STILL choose a smart contract on a public blockchain?
options: Reduces internal audit costs because the blockchain replaces the audit team entirely.
Cross-border interoperability — buyers in different jurisdictions can settle without correspondent banks, even if all are KYC'd.
Public blockchains are cheaper than databases for any volume of transactions.
Smart contracts cannot be hacked, so they're inherently more secure than the bank's database.
quiz.explanation: The first three options reflect common misconceptions: (1) blockchains do NOT replace audit functions — auditors still verify business logic, just with better evidence. (2) Public chains have ongoing gas costs that exceed databases for high-volume internal use. (3) Smart contracts are routinely hacked — security is a discipline, not a property. The legitimate reason in this scenario is cross-jurisdictional settlement: even with KYC'd parties, removing correspondent banks reduces friction, FX costs, and time. This is exactly why Santander chose Ethereum for their $20M bond. If the bond stayed entirely within one jurisdiction with one issuer and trusted buyers, a database would win — but the moment you span borders, blockchain unlocks real value.

takeaways.title: Section 04 — Key Takeaways
items: Not every problem needs blockchain — start by asking "who do we not trust?" If the answer is "no one," use a database
7 signals favoring smart contracts: multi-party, automation value, transparency, intermediary cost, disintermediation, immutability-as-feature, portable ownership
8 red flags: single trusted party, centralized is faster/cheaper, flexibility critical, off-chain data dominates, privacy paramount, hostile regulation, low user tech literacy, no clear ROI
Three field lessons — ✅ Estonia (right tool + right org), ⚠️ The DAO (right tool, wrong contract design), ❌ ASX CHESS (wrong tool entirely)
5-pillar adoption framework: Strategic Alignment · Technical Infrastructure · Legal & Regulatory · Risk Management · Financial Planning
Strategic: clear use case, success metrics, exec buy-in, staff training — without these, the project will stall
Legal: engage counsel BEFORE writing code. Securities classification, jurisdictional reach, GDPR all matter from day one
Risk: audits are non-optional. Bug bounties, formal verification, incident response — all baseline requirements
Cost reality: $50k–$500k+ to deploy a serious contract. Plan for ongoing gas, monitoring, and maintenance
Real strategic advantage comes from removing genuine intermediation pain — not from "doing blockchain"
